1: hôy hayyōrĕdîm miṣrayim lĕʿezrâ = "Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help (ʿezrâ)" — parallel to 30:1-2 but now focused specifically on military hardware. ʿal-sûsîm yiššāʿēnû = "who rely on horses (sûs)" — Egyptian cavalry was the most prized military commodity in the ancient Near East (cf. Deut 17:16, where the king is forbidden to multiply horses). kî rāḇ meʾōd ḥêlô ʿāṣûm mĕʾōd pārāšāyw = "for their army is very large, their cavalry very strong" — the human logic of military alliance: numbers and cavalry = security. wĕlōʾ šāʿû ʿal-qĕdôš yiśrāʾēl wĕʾet-YHWH lōʾ dāraš û = "but they do not look to the Holy One of Israel, and they do not seek YHWH" — the structural failure: military calculation without theological consultation.
2: wĕgam-hûʾ ḥāḵām wayyāḇēʾ rāʿ = "yet he too is wise and will bring disaster" — the sarcastic concession: YHWH is also "wise," but his wisdom produces judgment rather than deliverance for those who bypassed him. wĕlōʾ hēsîr dĕḇārāyw = "and he has not withdrawn his words" — YHWH's previous oracles of judgment remain in force; they are not canceled by the Egyptian alliance. wĕqām ʿal-bêt mĕrēʿîm wĕʿal-ʿezrat pōʿălê ʾāwen = "and he will rise against the house of evildoers and against the helpers of those who do iniquity." YHWH fights both against Israel (who sought the evil alliance) and against Egypt (who provided it).
3: wĕmiṣrayim ʾādām wĕlōʾ-ʾēl wĕsûsêhem bāśār wĕlōʾ-rûaḥ = "Egypt is human (ʾādām) and not God (ʾēl), their horses are flesh (bāśār) and not spirit (rûaḥ)" — the decisive theological contrast: the alliance partners (Egypt, horses) are located entirely in the category of the creaturely (ʾādām, bāśār) rather than the divine (ʾēl, rûaḥ). The contrast is ontological, not merely pragmatic. wĕYHWH yiṭṭeh yādô wĕkāšal ʿōzēr wĕnāpāl ʿāzûr wĕyaḥdāw kullām yikleyû = "and when YHWH stretches out his hand, the helper will stumble and the helped will fall, and all of them will perish together" — both Egypt and Judah collapse together when YHWH acts. The alliance that was supposed to be saving proves mutually destructive.
4: kî-kōh ʾāmar-lî YHWH kaʾăšer yehgeh hāʾaryēh wĕhakepir ʿal-ṭarpô = "For thus YHWH said to me: as the lion (ʾaryēh) growls (hāgâ) and the young lion (kepir) over his prey (ṭerep)" — YHWH is the lion standing over Jerusalem, not threatening it but protecting it. kî yiqqārēʾ ʿālāyw mĕlōʾ rōʿîm miqQôlam lōʾ yēḥāt = "when a multitude of shepherds is called out against him, he is not frightened (ḥātat) by their voice" — the lion is undaunted by the noise of human armies. YHWH as lion = both protector of Jerusalem (positive) and threat to enemies.
5: kĕṣipporîm ʿāpôt kēn yāgēn YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt ʿal-yĕrûšālaim = "Like hovering birds (ṣipporîm ʿāpôt), so YHWH of hosts will protect (gānan) Jerusalem" — two incompatible images in consecutive verses: lion (v4) and hovering birds (v5). The lion = fierce protection; the birds = delicate, attentive protection (cf. Deut 32:11: the eagle hovering over its young). Together they describe divine protection that is both powerful and tender. gānôn wĕhiṣṣîl pāsôaḥ wĕhimliṭ = "protecting and delivering, sparing and rescuing" — four verbs of deliverance paired asyndetically, creating urgency and comprehensiveness.
6: šûḇû lĕʾăšer heeʿemîqû sārâ bĕnê yiśrāʾēl = "Return (šûḇ) to the one from whom they have deeply revolted (heeʿămîqû sārâ)" — the call to repentance in the middle of the judgment-oracle. Heeʿămîqû = they have gone deep in revolt (the word of depth applied to their apostasy). The lion who protects is also the one from whom they fled; returning (šûḇ) to the protector is the only path to safety.
7: kî bĕyôm hahûʾ yimʾăsûn ʾîš ʾĕlîlêyw kesep wĕʾĕlîlêyw zāhāḇ = "For in that day everyone will reject (māʾas) his silver idols and golden idols" — the repentance expressed in concrete terms: abandonment of material religion. ʾăšer ʿāśû lākem yĕdêkem ḥēṭĕʾ = "which your own hands have made for you, sinfully" — the idols are the work of human hands (yĕdêkem), not the work of God; the distinction of 31:3 (human vs. divine, ʾādām vs. ʾēl) is applied to worship objects as well as military allies.
8: wĕnāpal ʾAššûr bĕḥereḇ lōʾ-ʾîš wĕḥereḇ lōʾ-ʾādām tōʾklennû = "And Assyria will fall by the sword, but not of man (ʾîš); a sword, but not of man (ʾādām), will devour him" — the double negation is crucial: Assyria's defeat will not come through Egyptian cavalry or human military power (the very thing Judah sought) but through direct divine action. wĕnās mippĕnê-ḥāreḇ ûbaḥûrāyw lāmas yihyû = "and he will flee from the sword and his young men will become forced labor (mas)" — the military elite that was Assyria's pride reduced to corvée workers.
9: wĕsalʿô mimmāgôr yaʿăḇōr = "and his rock (sela) will pass away in terror (māgôr)" — Assyria's stronghold (either a deity or military fortress) will crumble in fear. wĕḥaṭṭāʾû ʾet-nisSô miyyîrāʾ = "and his officers will be terrified at the battle standard" — the commanders who rallied troops to the standard (nēs) will flee from it. nĕʾum YHWH ʾăšer-ûr lô bĕṣiyyôn wĕtannûr lô bîrûšālaim = "declares YHWH, whose fire (ʾûr) is in Zion and whose furnace (tannûr) is in Jerusalem" — the closing declaration: the fire of divine judgment is located in Zion. It destroys the enemy (Assyria) while purifying the city (Jerusalem).
